topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the coffee shop felt thick, pressing against my eardrums. I watched Liam across the small table, his easy laughter directed at the barista, a sound that used to be the soundtrack to my entire adolescence. Now, it was a perfectly tuned melody I couldn't join. I traced the rim of my mug, the ceramic cool beneath my fingertips, a small, useless gesture against the vast emptiness where shared history used to reside. Every casual glance he spared my way was a deliberate erasure. I tried to speak, to offer a familiar inside joke, but the words caught in my throat, lodging there like grit. I pulled my jacket tighter around myself, a futile attempt to build a barrier against the chilling realization that I existed in a parallel timeline, one where he simply didn't see the person I was. My shoulders slumped, a slow, heavy surrender to the solitude.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the memory—the disastrous camping trip, the secret we swore we’d never tell anyone. It felt like a phantom limb, aching with the ghost of a connection that had been severed. When Liam finally turned to me, his expression was one of polite, distant recognition, like acknowledging a stranger who shared a brief acquaintance. His eyes flickered over me, registering my presence, but nothing sparked. A tightness coiled in my chest, a physical constriction that made drawing a deep breath an effort. I gripped my phone so hard my knuckles bleached white. I wanted to shout, to demand that he *remember*, but the sound died before it could form, leaving only a dry rasp in my throat. I began methodically gathering my things, the movements stiff and unnatural, as if navigating a landscape where the familiar landmarks had suddenly vanished beneath shifting sand.

[story 3]

The hallway felt endless, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an irritating, relentless energy. I stood by the lockers, my backpack heavy on my shoulders, feeling the weight of every unshared moment pressing down. I’d rehearsed the conversation a hundred times in my head—a simple greeting, a shared reference, anything to bridge the chasm that had opened between us. But the words felt hollow, echoing in the sterile air. When Liam finally approached, his smile was warm, genuine, directed at someone else entirely. He offered a casual nod, a gesture so light it felt like a deliberate push away. I watched him walk past, his silhouette dissolving into the crowd, and a profound stillness settled over me. My feet felt rooted to the linoleum floor, unable to move toward him, unable to move away. I just stood there, absorbing the fact that the foundation of our shared world had crumbled without a sound.
